mappings.rationaleAI: Alexander's conquest of the Persian Empire and his governing practices directly address the empire of Alexander the Great (B.2.h) and trigger the Hellenistic Age (C).

Augustus's establishment of the principate, professional army, Praetorian Guard, and initiation of the Pax Romana directly address consolidation of the empire under the Julio-Claudians (E.1) and Augustus's establishment of the principate (E.1.a).
Phoenician Carthage's conflicts with Rome connect to relations among Hellenistic states including the Punic Wars context (C.2.d).
Ancient Rome's constitutional evolution from monarchy through republic to empire comprehensively addresses the rise of Rome (D) and the Roman Empire (E).
Caesar's Gallic Wars, civil war, dictatorship, and assassination directly address the alliance of Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus and Caesar's conquest and assassination (D.5.e).
The Achaemenid Empire's conflicts with Greece connect to the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii).
The Byzantine Empire's origins in Constantine's establishment of Constantinople and Diocletian's reforms connect to the recovery of the Roman Empire and establishment of the dominate (E.4.e).
Ptolemaic Egypt as a Hellenistic kingdom connects to Macedonian and Ptolemaic Egypt (C.1.b). Its annexation under Octavian relates to the establishment of sole power (D.5.f).
Cleopatra VII as last Ptolemaic ruler and her alliances with Caesar and Antony directly address the Ptolemaic dynasty ending with her death (C.1.b.i) and Octavian's annexation of Egypt (D.5.f).
Ancient history from writing through late antiquity broadly addresses all areas of Section 912.
Ancient Greece's Archaic, Classical, and Hellenistic periods comprehensively address archaic Greece and Classical Greek civilization (B).

Cicero's consulship, suppression of Catiline, and political role in the late Republic directly address growing political suspicion and the conspiracies of Catiline (D.5.d.ii). His philosophy and oratory relate to culture in the late republic (D.5.h).
The spread of Bronze Age industry throughout Europe by c. 1500 BC directly addresses Bronze Age developments in Europe (A.2.b).
The Iron Age in Europe from c. 650 BC directly addresses non-Classical Europe in the Iron Age (A.3).
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey directly address the world of Homer and Hesiod during the Geometric Period (B.1.b).
Socrates' trial and execution in Athens directly relate to the Sophists and Socrates during the Peloponnesian War era (B.2.c).
Italy's Italic prehistory and the Etruscans connect to the Etruscans and other Italic peoples (A.3.a) and the rise of Rome (D).
Rome as the capital of the Roman Kingdom, Republic, and Empire broadly connects to the rise of Rome (D) and the Roman Empire (E).
Cyrus as founder of the Achaemenid Empire whose successors clashed with Greece provides context for the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii).
The spread of Neolithic farming communities throughout Europe by c. 2000 BC directly addresses Europe before the Iron Age (A.2.a).
Europe's classical antiquity and prehistoric cultures broadly connect to non-Classical ancient Europe (A).
The history of Greek and Roman art connects to the arts in the Archaic Period (B.1.c.vii) and Greco-Roman culture (E.3.b).
The post-classical transition from the Roman Empire connects to the provinces under the later empire and Germanic hegemony (E.4.g).
